name: The arrest in the garden
notes: All four Gospels recount Jesus' arrest in the garden across the brook Cedron, and their shared core runs in close parallel: Judas comes leading a great multitude or band with swords and staves from the chief priests, the betrayer's hand is laid on Jesus to take him, a sword is drawn and the servant of the high priest loses his ear, and Jesus answers his captors. Matthew and Mark stand nearest, agreeing nearly word for word on the kiss as the appointed sign, the rebuke 'Are ye come out as against a thief,' the reminder that he sat daily teaching in the temple, and the disciples all forsaking him and fleeing; Luke joins this synoptic block in the rebuke and the temple word, adding 'this is your hour, and the power of darkness.' Each account keeps its own vantage: Matthew alone records 'Friend, wherefore art thou come' and 'they that take the sword shall perish'; John supplies the complementary scene of 'Whom seek ye,' Jesus' 'I am he,' and the band falling backward, names Peter and Malchus where the synoptics leave the swordsman and servant unnamed, and Luke alone records the healing of the ear. Mark's young man fleeing naked is unique to his account. These differing details are complementary vantages on the one event, the fuller telling drawn from many eyes; the precise naming and the healing are each kept by a single writer and read in place.
